So, the code would look like:

class Solution {
public:
    int longestPalindrome(string s, string t) {
        vector<string> sSubs = generateSubstrings(s);
        vector<string> tSubs = generateSubstrings(t);
        int max_len = 0;
        for (const string& s_sub : sSubs) {
            for (const string& t_sub : tSubs) {
                string combined = s_sub + t_sub;
                if (isPalindrome(combined)) {
                    if (combined.length() > max_len) {
                        max_len = combined.length();
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return max_len;
    }

private:
    static bool isPalindrome(const string& s) {
        int n = s.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < n / 2; ++i) {
            if (s[i] != s[n - 1 - i]) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }

    static vector<string> generateSubstrings(const string& s) {
        vector<string> subs;
        int n = s.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            for (int j = i; j < n; ++j) {
                subs.push_back(s.substr(i, j - i + 1));
            }
        }
        subs.push_back("");
        return subs;
    }
};

Time complexity: For each string, generating substrings is O(n^2), which is 30^2 = 900. So for two strings, it's 2*900 = 1800 steps.

Then, for each pair of substrings, which is 900 * 900 = 810,000 pairs, each requiring a palindrome check of up to 60 characters. So, 810,000 * 60 = 48,600,000 operations. That's acceptable.

Space complexity: Storing all substrings for s and t. Each has up to 900 substrings, each of length up to 30. So, for s, 900 * 30 = 27,000 characters, same for t. So total space is about 54,000 characters, which is manageable.

So, the code should work efficiently.

Another possible optimization: precompute all possible palindromic substrings for s and t, and then find the maximum combined length. But I think the initial approach is sufficient.

So, the final code is as written above.

solution: 
```class Solution {
public:
    int longestPalindrome(string s, string t) {
        vector<string> sSubs = generateSubstrings(s);
        vector<string> tSubs = generateSubstrings(t);
        int max_len = 0;
        for (const string& s_sub : sSubs) {
            for (const string& t_sub : tSubs) {
                string combined = s_sub + t_sub;
                if (isPalindrome(combined)) {
                    if (combined.length() > max_len) {
                        max_len = combined.length();
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return max_len;
    }

private:
    static bool isPalindrome(const string& s) {
        int n = s.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < n / 2; ++i) {
            if (s[i] != s[n - 1 - i]) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }

    static vector<string> generateSubstrings(const string& s) {
        vector<string> subs;
        int n = s.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            for (int j = i; j < n; ++j) {
                subs.push_back(s.substr(i, j - i + 1));
            }
        }
        subs.push_back("");
        return subs;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O((m^2 + n^2) + (m^2 * n^2) * k), where m and n are the lengths of s and t, and k is the average length of the concatenated substrings.
space complexity: O(m^2 + n^2)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution efficiently generates all possible substrings for both input strings and checks each combination to find the longest palindrome. The approach ensures that all edge cases, including empty substrings, are considered, providing a comprehensive solution within acceptable time and space limits for the given problem constraints.
